RULING:
DEFENDANT DANA COMPANIES, LLC SUED HEREIN AS DANA COMPANIES' MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

No other appearance and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Dana Companies, LLC's motion for summary adjudication of plaintiff's claim for punitive damages is denied. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, clear and convincing evidence that defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. Plaintiff's responses to defendant's special interrogatories preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, matter not reported (503/CML)

Defendant George Baba'S Motion For Summary Judgment (Part 2 Of 2)
Off Calendar Mar-08-2017 Text Ruling

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT By Defendant George Paul Baba Sued Herein As Doe 301.
Off Calendar Mar-08-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES:

George P. Eshoo, Esq. of LAW OFFICES OF GEORGE P. ESHOO AND ASSOCIATES (650.364.7030), appearing for defendant / moving party George Paul Baba (sued herein as DOE 301).

Garen Nazarian, Esq. of BRAYTON PURCELL LLP (415.898.1555), appearing for plaintiff Larry Walker.

RULING: Defendant George Baba's Motion For Summary Judgment.

Matter called, argument held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as follows: Defendant George Paul Baba's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to demonstrate that plaintiff's claim is precluded by the Workers' Compensation exclusivity rule, as defendant concedes that plaintiff was not employed by defendant during the time(s) at issue. Defendant failed to demonstrate that plaintiff's claim is precluded by the doctrine set forth in Privette v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 689 and its progeny. Assuming that defendant did not retain control over plaintiff and/or plaintiff's work and that there was no concealed preexisting hazardous condition on the property, defendant still cannot demonstrate that plaintiff's Negligence II cause of action must fail. Plaintiff's claims are based on the premise that the actions of defendant's employees affirmatively contributed to plaintiff's exposure to asbestos. (See Biles v. Exxon Mobil Corp. (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 1315.) Notwithstanding this conclusion, defendant failed to sustain its initial burden on its Privette argument under either prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Defendant is precluded from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar because it failed to demonstrate that plaintiff failed to provide meaningful responses to comprehensive interrogatories designed to elicit all the evidence plaintiff has to support his contention of liability. Defendant also failed to submit evidence sufficient to negate the duty element of plaintiff's cause of action. Mr. Baba's declaration is not sufficient to do so. Assuming defendant properly raised the issue of threshold exposure/causation, defendant failed to sustain its initial burden on this issue. Defendant is precluded from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar because it failed to demonstrate that plaintiff failed to provide meaningful responses to comprehensive interrogatories designed to elicit all the evidence plaintiff has to support his contention of liability. Defendant also failed to submit evidence sufficient to negate the causation element of plaintiff's cause of action. Mr. Baba's declaration is not sufficient to do so. At most, Mr. Baba's declaration, when read in conjunction with plaintiff's deposition testimony, creates a triable issue of material fact whether the workers/laborers at issue were employed by defendant. Plaintiff's objections to the declaration of George Baba: Objection no. 1 is sustained as to "There was no asbestos on the construction site," and overruled as to "and I was not aware of any asbestos being used." Objections no. 2 and no. 3 are overruled. Objection no. 4 is sustained. The Court did not consider defendant's reply evidence. (Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243; San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Court Reporter: Leticia A. Ralls of AikenWelch, CSR# 10070, (877.451.1580). (503/CML)
